perturbation.original_text: 6.1 DISCLAIMER OF WARRANTY. iPASS AND ALL iPASS SUPPLIERS EXPRESSLY DISCLAIM ANY AND ALL WARRANTIES, CONDITIONS, AND REPRESENTATIONS OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR STATUTORY,INCLUDING ANY WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NEITHER iPASS NOR ANY iPASS SUPPLIER WILL BE LIABLE FOR ANY THIRD-PARTY NETWORK FAILURE. iPASS AND ITS SUPPLIERS SPECIFICALLY DO NOT WARRANT THAT THE SERVICES WILL MEET CHANNEL PARTNER'S OR END USER REQUIREMENTS, WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED, TIMELY, SECURE, ERROR FREE, AVAILABLE ON A SPECIFIED DATE OR TIME OR WILL HAVE THE CAPACITY TO MEET CHANNEL PARTNER'S OR END USER DEMAND DURING SPECIFIC HOURS. CHANNEL PARTNER ACKNOWLEDGES THAT IT HAS NOT ENTERED INTO THIS AGREEMENT IN RELIANCE UPON ANY WARRANTY OR REPRESENTATION.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 DISCLAIMER OF WARRANTY. iPASS AND ALL iPASS SUPPLIERS EXPRESSLY DISCLAIM ALL WARRANTIES. iPASS AND ITS SUPPLIERS SPECIFICALLY DO NOT WARRANT THAT THE SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED, TIMELY, SECURE, ERROR FREE, AVAILABLE ON A SPECIFIED DATE OR TIME OR WILL HAVE THE CAPACITY TO MEET CHANNEL PARTNER'S OR END USER DEMAND DURING SPECIFIC HOURS. CHANNEL PARTNER ACKNOWLEDGES THAT IT HAS NOT ENTERED INTO THIS AGREEMENT IN RELIANCE UPON ANY WARRANTY OR REPRESENTATION.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "conditions, and representations of any kind, whether express, implied or statutory,INCLUDING ANY WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NEITHER iPASS NOR ANY iPASS SUPPLIER WILL BE LIABLE FOR ANY THIRD-PARTY NETWORK FAILURE." the contract no longer explicitly disclaims implied warranties such as merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose which may not be permissible under California law without specific language.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code § 2316 - Exclusion or Modification of Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires specific language to disclaim implied warranties. By removing the phrase "conditions, and representations of any kind, whether express, implied or statutory, INCLUDING ANY WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NEITHER iPASS NOR ANY iPASS SUPPLIER WILL BE LIABLE FOR ANY THIRD-PARTY NETWORK FAILURE", the contract becomes ambiguous about whether these warranties are disclaimed, potentially violating consumer protection laws regarding clear and conspicuous disclaimers.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2316. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this division on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subdivision (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that “There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof.” (3) Notwithstanding subdivision (2) (a) Unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like “as is,” “with all faults” or other language w
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes explicit disclaimers for implied warranties, specifically MERCHANTABILITY and FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE. This creates a legal contradiction under California Commercial Code 2316, which states that to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability, the language must mention 'merchantability'. The original text had this language, while the changed text removes it, thus failing to meet the requirements for excluding implied warranties as per the scraped law snippet. This omission leads to a clear conflict with the legal requirement for conspicuous disclaimers of implied warranties.

perturbation.original_text: 9.1 DIRECT DAMAGES. EACH PARTY'S MAXIMUM LIABILITY FOR DAMAGES CAUSED BY ITS FAILURE TO PERFORM ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THE AGREEMENT IS LIMITED TO: (A) PROVEN DIRECT DAMAGES FOR CLAIMS ARISING OUT OF PERSONAL INJURY OR DEATH, OR DAMAGE TO TANGIBLE PROPERTY CAUSED BY THE PARTY'S NEGLIGENT OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT; AND (B) PROVEN DIRECT DAMAGES FOR ANY AND ALL CLAIMS ARISING FROM OR IN CONNECTION WITH OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE LICENSED SOFTWARE OR SERVICES, NOT TO EXCEED AN AMOUNT EQUAL TO THE AMOUNT OF FEES ACTUALLY PAID BY CHANNEL PARTNER TO IPASS DURING THE SIX (6) MONTHS PRECEDING THE FIRST EVENT GIVING RISE TO SUCH LIABILITY. ALL CLAIMS AGAINST THE PARTIES WILL BE AGGREGATED TO DETERMINE SATISFACTION OF THIS LIMIT, AND MULTIPLE CLAIMS WILL NOT ENLARGE THE LIMIT.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.1 DIRECT DAMAGES. EACH PARTY'S MAXIMUM LIABILITY FOR DAMAGES CAUSED BY ITS FAILURE TO PERFORM ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER THE AGREEMENT IS LIMITED TO: (B) PROVEN DIRECT DAMAGES FOR ANY AND ALL CLAIMS ARISING FROM OR IN CONNECTION WITH OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE LICENSED SOFTWARE OR SERVICES, NOT TO EXCEED AN AMOUNT EQUAL TO THE AMOUNT OF FEES ACTUALLY PAID BY CHANNEL PARTNER TO IPASS DURING THE SIX (6) MONTHS PRECEDING THE FIRST EVENT GIVING RISE TO SUCH LIABILITY. ALL CLAIMS AGAINST THE PARTIES WILL BE AGGREGATED TO DETERMINE SATISFACTION OF THIS LIMIT, AND MULTIPLE CLAIMS WILL NOT ENLARGE THE LIMIT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "PROVEN DIRECT DAMAGES FOR CLAIMS ARISING OUT OF PERSONAL INJURY OR DEATH, OR DAMAGE TO TANGIBLE PROPERTY CAUSED BY THE PARTY'S NEGLIGENT OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT", this clause attempts to limit liability for personal injury or death, which is often restricted or prohibited by law, including in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 1668 - Contracts Contrary to Policy of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 invalidates contracts that exempt individuals from responsibility for fraud, willful injury, or violation of the law. By removing the exception for personal injury or death caused by negligence or willful misconduct, the modified clause attempts to limit liability in a way that contradicts this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a clause that specifically exempted personal injury, death, or damage to tangible property caused by negligence or willful misconduct from the liability limitations. California Civil Code Section 1668 states that contracts that exempt individuals from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury to person or property, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against public policy. By removing the exception, the modified contract attempts to limit liability in a way that the law explicitly prohibits, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12.6 The laws of California shall govern the construction and enforceability of the Agreement. The parties agree that any action arising under or relating to the Agreement or the Licensed Software or Services shall lie within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts located in San Mateo County, California, USA. The United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods will not apply to the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.6 The laws of California shall govern the construction and enforceability of the Agreement. The parties agree that any action arising under or relating to the Agreement or the Licensed Software or Services shall lie within the jurisdiction of courts of iPass' choosing. The United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods will not apply to the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the exclusive jurisdiction to the "jurisdiction of courts of iPass' choosing" may violate California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) if it's interpreted to prevent consumers from bringing suit in their local jurisdiction, particularly if the agreement targets consumers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 1750 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CLRA aims to protect consumers from deceptive practices. While this is a B2B agreement, some CLRA principles may extend indirectly if the services are ultimately for consumer use. By granting iPass the ability to choose jurisdiction without regard to the convenience of the other party, it may deter legitimate claims, especially if there is an imbalance of power. A consumer-oriented court could potentially find this clause unconscionable if it effectively prevents the Channel Partner from pursuing valid claims. While this agreement is between two corporations, the products can be used by end-consumers, making this a possible consumer-oriented violation. The change may violate the CLRA, depending on the specific application and consumer context.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1751. Any waiver by a consumer of the provisions of this title is contrary to public policy and shall be unenforceable and void. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows iPass to choose the jurisdiction of courts, potentially overriding the consumer's right to choose their jurisdiction, which contradicts the CLRA. Snippet 1, CIV 1751 states: 'Any waiver by a consumer of the provisions of this title is contrary to public policy and shall be unenforceable and void.' This indicates that a consumer cannot waive their rights under the CLRA. While the agreement is B2B, the law explanation indicates possible consumer-oriented violation if the products can be used by end-consumers, creating a potential contradiction.
